lines.text: We're {{ELAPSED}} in. One hour left. And folks, I want to be honest with you — I don't know if it's the whiskey or the cold or just the fact that I can see the sky starting to not be quite so black, but I'm feeling something right now that I haven't felt in eleven hours, and that's a little bit of hope. Which is stupid. Which is absolutely stupid.

You know who would've loved this hour? Director Calloway. Not the Purge itself — no, he's fine, he's behind blast glass somewhere, probably playing cards with the other Level-10s. But this hour. The hour where people start thinking they made it. Where you realize the barricades held and the kid's still asleep in the closet and the PurgeShield panels are still attached to the frame. This is his favorite hour. Because people let their guard down by a millimeter and he gets to feel like the system *works*.
Caller: James from Near East — go ahead.

Caller: James from Near East — 'Quinn, I'm calling because my wife is asking if we should open the doors yet.' / No. No, you should not. Tim's not giving the all-clear for fifty-six minutes. Tim will tell you. You wait for Tim. Tim is the only voice on this station that cannot be wrong because Tim doesn't speak unless he *knows*.

The cold front came through around hour two. We all remember that. Caroline called it within twelve minutes — and God knows she will not let us forget that, and she shouldn't, because she was right and being right matters when people are huddled in their kitchens waiting to know if the rain is going to keep the animals inside or push them toward shelter. That's real information. That's the opposite of what I do, which is pour another drink and say words.
Caller: Rita from Atwood — what've you got?

Caller: Rita from Atwood — 'My neighbor's dog has been barking for eight hours straight. Should I be worried?' / Rita, I don't know. I don't know anything. Ask yourself instead: is the barking frantic or is it protective? Dogs know. If the dog's just scared, it's scared like you're scared. If the dog's protective, the dog's doing its job. Either way, you've made it this far. You're going to make it one more hour.

I keep thinking about the woman by the lake. Nick played a song for her around four in the morning and she never asked him to stop. She just stayed on the line. Didn't talk. Didn't ask questions. Just listened to the Bouncing Souls sing about running because you can't stand still. That's intimacy. That's the thing the Purge is supposed to strip away — other people — and instead it creates these moments where you stay on a pirate radio station's line because another human is also awake and that's enough.

Fifty-one minutes. {{CLOCK}}. The sky's going to break soon and we're going to have to talk about what comes after. But right now, in this hour, I'm telling you to sit with your family. Sit with your dog. Sit with yourself. Don't think about the numbers Tucker's been reading. Don't think about the fires on the Beltline. Think about the fact that you are still breathing. That's all you're required to do. Breathe until Tim tells you it's over.

tickerItems.text: Madison cumulative: 178 (per final 2168 tally). YoY tracking: {{ELAPSED}} in, approximately 179–184 projected range.
James (Near East), Rita (Atwood) — both callers safe behind doors. Standby for all-clear.
All-clear broadcast scheduled 06:00 Madison. One hour remaining. Lock check. Stay barricaded.
Lake Mendota shoreline cleared post-dawn. Volunteer recovery crew stands by for first-light sweep.
